behaviors.description: Cache keys generated from identical query parameters are always identical, and keys from different parameters never collide.
behaviors.justification: Cache key generation is a pure deterministic function with no I/O, external state, or timing dependencies. Real failures (wrong hash, collisions) are caught by calling the function multiple times with known inputs and verifying deterministic output. Mocking cannot hide this failure because the behavior is entirely local.
behaviors.test_description: Call the key generation function multiple times with identical parameter sets (e.g., {'user_id': 123, 'category': 'profile'}) and verify the key is always the same. Call with different parameters and verify different keys are produced. Test edge cases: parameter order variation, special characters, empty parameters, and hash boundary conditions.

behaviors.description: The TTL for a cache entry is computed correctly based on the data category's configured freshness rule.
behaviors.justification: TTL computation maps a category name to a configured freshness interval—a pure function. If configuration is loaded once and available to the computation function, Unit testing suffices: provide known category-to-TTL mappings and verify correct TTL is returned. Real failure (wrong interval applied) is caught without external I/O. However, if configuration is fetched dynamically from a database on each call, Integration becomes necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Create test fixtures with known category-to-TTL mappings (e.g., 'user_profiles' → 300 seconds, 'product_catalog' → 3600 seconds). Call the TTL computation function for each category and verify correct TTL is returned. Test edge cases: unknown categories, missing configurations, zero/negative TTLs, and boundary values (minimum/maximum allowed TTL).

behaviors.description: When memory usage exceeds the configured threshold, the eviction strategy switches from LRU to LFU as specified in the configuration.
behaviors.justification: Detecting memory pressure and switching strategies requires testing with actual cache state under realistic load. Unit testing with mocked memory measurements would hide real failures: the actual memory detection might not work, or the real data might cause memory pressure in unexpected ways. Integration testing with a real cache populated with realistic entries ensures both the memory detection and the strategy-switching logic work correctly under genuine conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Create an Integration test that programmatically inserts cache entries until actual memory usage reaches (or slightly exceeds) the configured threshold. Verify the eviction strategy has switched from LRU to LFU by checking the cache's internal state or configuration. Assert that subsequent evictions follow LFU rules (removing least-frequently-used entries). Include edge cases: memory just below threshold (should not switch), exactly at threshold (should switch), and rapid insertions exceeding threshold (strategy should switch and stay switched).

behaviors.description: A write to the underlying PostgreSQL table invalidates all cache entries whose keys match the affected rows.
behaviors.justification: Cache invalidation triggered by database writes requires a real (or test instance of) PostgreSQL to verify the end-to-end mechanism works. The test must perform an actual write to Postgres and verify the cache correctly detects and removes matching entries. Mocking the Postgres write would hide real failures in the invalidation trigger, the row-to-key matching logic, or the cache deletion mechanism itself. A local test PostgreSQL instance (Integration) provides the minimum infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test PostgreSQL database. Insert cache entries corresponding to specific rows (e.g., user_id=42). Perform a database write (INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE) that affects those rows. Verify that corresponding cache entries are no longer retrievable from the cache. Test multiple scenarios: single-row updates, multi-row updates via WHERE clause, cascading deletes. Include edge cases: writes to unrelated tables (cache unaffected), writes to related rows but different query parameters (cache unaffected), and concurrent writes and cache reads.

behaviors.description: The eviction policy removes the correct entries—least-recently-used under LRU, least-frequently-used under LFU—when the cache is full.
behaviors.justification: Verifying correct eviction requires the cache to actually track access recency (LRU timestamps) and frequency (LFU counts) through real cache operations. Unit testing with mocked cache state cannot detect failures in how timestamps or frequency counts are maintained during actual accesses. Integration testing that populates the cache, performs specific access patterns, triggers eviction, and verifies the correct entries are removed will catch real failures in both the eviction algorithm and the access-tracking mechanism.
behaviors.test_description: Create an Integration test that inserts N cache entries, then performs a specific access pattern (e.g., frequently access entries A and B, rarely access C and D), fills the cache to trigger eviction, and verifies the evicted entries are the least-recently-used (LRU mode) or least-frequently-used (LFU mode) entries, not others. Test both LRU and LFU modes in separate tests. Include edge cases: evicting when cache is exactly full (should evict one entry), evicting with tied recency/frequency (eviction order must be deterministic), single-entry cache, and no accesses since insertion (all entries equally old/unused).
